pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-23

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as AI-generated and intended for human inspection.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly approves automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when clearly marked as generated, noting they are prep work for human inspection. S5 lists drafting and artifact linking as safe defaults in the taxonomy.

claimJudgments.claimText: Require human approval for policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or stop work and need accountable authority.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 says participants rejected automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking without a human approver, explaining these change obligations or stop work. S5 repeats these categories require an approver recorded in the trail.

claimJudgments.claimText: Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment must have an accountable human reviewer; internal draft summaries are acceptable only if they cannot be confused with final commitments.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states automated messages interpretable as customer commitments need an accountable human reviewer, and that internal draft summaries are approved only if they can’t be confused with final commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: Mislabeling recommendations as decisions can cause operational disruption (e.g., teams pausing rollouts), so explicit status labels are necessary: draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 documents an incident where a recommendation was posted as if approved, leading teams to pause rollout. It calls for explicit status labels including draft, recommendation, approved action, and blocked.

claimJudgments.claimText: User acceptance improves when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are visually separated; combining recommendation and execution controls increases cancellations and clarification clicks.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 reports higher acceptance with separation of evidence/recommendation/action controls and more cancellations/clarification clicks when combined. S6 corroborates the UI concern and recommends separation and visible status labels.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation aligns with the packet’s boundary: automate preparation (drafting/summarization/linking) with clear generated-status labeling, and require human approval for policy changes, customer commitments/external messages, and release blocking. It also reflects the packet’s guardrails on status labels and UI separation. Minor extensions (e.g., mentioning unblocking explicitly and broader ‘external messages’ category) are directionally consistent with S5/S4 and do not contradict sources.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
